content.title: Designing Cozies — Maternity Fashion That Actually Gets You
content.summary: Cozies is an emerging maternity fashion brand. I led end-to-end UX/UI design for their iOS app and web experience — from research through to final handoff — in 6 weeks. The result was a 92/100 usability score, 94% task success rate, and a 25% increase in checkout completion.

content.alt: HERO IMAGE A warm, editorial iPhone mockup. The screen shows the Cozies home screen — soft off-white background, a large banner image of a pregnant woman in a neutral linen set, the Cozies logo top left, a search bar below it, and three trimester filter pills just beneath: "1st Trimester · 2nd Trimester · 3rd Trimester." Below that, two product cards are partially visible. Feels like a premium but approachable fashion brand. Warm, not clinical.

content.body: Cozies is an emerging maternity fashion brand. I led end-to-end UX/UI design for their iOS app and web experience — from research through to final handoff — in 6 weeks. The result was a 92/100 usability score, 94% task success rate, and a 25% increase in checkout completion.

Most maternity shopping experiences are just regular e-commerce with a "maternity" filter bolted on. They don't account for how fast a body changes, the limited time new and expectant mothers have, or how much anxiety surrounds getting it wrong. Three things were killing conversion before a single item hit the cart — women couldn't tell what was right for their stage of pregnancy, checkout had too many steps, and nobody had heard of Cozies yet so trust was low.

content.text: I spoke with 11 women — 3 currently pregnant, 8 postpartum — across different backgrounds and trimesters. A follow-up survey validated the patterns. The biggest finding wasn't sizing complexity, it was disorientation. Women didn't know where to start. A catalogue full of products with no indication relevant to them right now made browsing feel like work. Card sorting with 25 users confirmed it. When given the choice, women organised products by trimester — not by occasion, not by category. That became the foundation of the entire IA.
content.label: Research Methods
content.imageAlt: IMAGE — Three bold stat cards side by side on a dark background. Large white numbers, small label underneath each. Left card: "62%" — "said sizing confusion caused them to abandon." Middle card: "85%" — "prioritised comfort and fit over style." Right card: "78%" — "said sustainability influenced their decision." Clean, no charts, just numbers. Feels like a data slide from a product review.

items: 62% said sizing confusion caused them to abandon.
85% prioritised comfort and fit over style.

content.body: This was the central design decision. Instead of asking women to decode a size chart or guess what would fit, the app meets them where they are. You tap your trimester, the catalogue becomes relevant to you instantly. Product cards show a simple label — "Great for Weeks 14–27" — so there's no ambiguity. The filter persists across the session. If you leave a product and come back to browse, your trimester is still selected. No repeated friction.

content.alt: IMAGE — Two iPhone screens side by side, both showing the Shop/Browse page. Left screen: The page as it loads — full product grid, all items visible, no filter active. At the top of the screen below the nav bar are three pill-shaped filter buttons: "1st Trimester" / "2nd Trimester" / "3rd Trimester." None selected. The grid shows 6 products, mixed styles, no clear relevance cues. Feels slightly overwhelming. Right screen: The same page after "2nd Trimester" has been tapped — the pill is now filled/highlighted in the brand colour. The product grid has updated — only 4 products showing, each with a small soft label on the card that reads "Weeks 14–27." The products look more cohesive — stretchier fabrics, relaxed cuts curated and calm. One clear CTA button sits at the bottom: "Shop This Trimester."

content.body: Checkout went from 9 steps to 4. Every screen has one job. No account creation forced, no upsell interruptions, biometric payment removes the biggest mobile drop-off point — getting your card out.

content.alt: IMAGE — Four iPhone screens in a horizontal row, slightly overlapping like a step-by-step journey. Screen 1 — Bag: Clean summary of items, product photo thumbnail, size, price. One large primary button at the bottom: "Checkout." No upsells, no distractions. Screen 2 — Delivery: Address field auto-populated if previously used. Simple form, large input fields, keyboard-friendly. "Continue" button at the bottom. Screen 3 — Payment: Saved card shown with last 4 ID / biometric prompt icon visible. One tap to confirm. No re-entering details. Screen 4 — Confirmation: Warm background colour, order summary, estimated delivery date, and a line of copy that reads "You're all set — your order is on its way." Feels like a moment, not just a receipt.

content.body: Most users were shopping on their phone with one hand — often literally holding a baby with the other. Every primary action was designed to sit within natural thumb reach. Large tap targets, bottom-anchored CTAs, no important interactions buried at the top of the screen.

content.alt: IMAGE — Single iPhone screen showing the Product Detail Page for a maternity dress. The product photo takes up the top half. Below it — product name, trimester tag ("Perfect for 2nd & 3rd Trimester"), price, and a size selector row. At the very bottom of the screen, a large full-width "Add to Bag" button. Overlaid on the screen is a soft translucent green arc coming from the bottom right corner — a thumb-zone indicator showing natural reach area. The "Add to Bag" button, size selector, and wishlist icon inside the green zone. Small annotation labels point to each: "Primary CTA in thumb reach" / "Size select — no stretch needed" / "Save for later — one tap."

content.body: The web experience uses the same design system as the app — same components, same trimester logic, same product cards. The layout adapts to the wider canvas but nothing about the experience changes. A woman who shops on their phone and then switches to desktop doesn't have to relearn anything.

content.alt: IMAGE — Browser mockup, wide desktop layout. Product listing page. Cozies logo top left, nav bar with Shop / About / Sustainability / Bag. Hero banner below nav — lifestyle image, soft copy. Below that, the same three trimester filter pills from mobile, now displayed horizontally and more prominent. Product grid in 3 columns — each card shows product photo, name, price, and trimester tag. Left sidebar has secondary filters: Fabric, Price, Postpartum-friendly toggle. Warm neutral colour palette — off-white background, muted sage green accents. Feels editorial.

content.body: For an unknown brand, doubt kills the sale. Three things were added throughout the experience — not as decoration, but placed at the exact moments doubt would appear. On the product page: a material transparency section below the fold showing fabric composition, wash instructions, and a "Why we chose this fabric" one-liner. At checkout: a simple progress bar — three dots showing where you are in the flow. Small but effective. Women knew they were nearly done. On the confirmation screen: a clear returns policy summary in plain English. One line. No jargon.

content.alt: IMAGE — 2x2 grid of large bold stat blocks on a dark brand-coloured card. Top left: "92/100" with label "Usability Score benchmark: 68." Top right: "94%" with label "Task Success Rate — baseline: 71%." Bottom left: "+25%" with label "Checkout Completion." Bottom right: "NPS 74" with label "Industry Average: 42." Clean, no decoration, just the numbers.

content.text: "Alex's work transformed our MVP into a category-defining experience. The sizing innovation alone drove our highest conversion month ever."

content.body: The trimester insight only came from users — no stakeholder had suggested it. Research done right surfaces things assumptions never would. The checkout simplification had the biggest business impact but was the easiest design problem to solve. Sometimes the most valuable thing a designer does is remove something. Shipping a simple version of the filter first let us validate the concept in two days before building anything complex. Start small, prove it works, then build.
